title: Where Am I
html:
A lot of people say heartbreak is the most painful thing a person can feel.

They say you can't fix it by popping a few pills, and honestly, that tracks. But oh my fucking god, where the fuck am I?

A few minutes ago I was at work, watching a crying woman sob into her wine after finding out her husband was cheating. Honestly entertaining. A live drama playing out between entrées, and I can say that without much empathy since I've never been close enough to someone to feel what she's feeling.

That was a moment ago. Right now I'm in a sealed room with around a hundred strangers and no memory of how I got here. Some of them are shouting, a few are already crying, and the air is going heavy with too many people in not enough space. The walls are solid on all four sides and I can't find a door or a vent. The oxygen situation is going to become a problem.

Am I getting kidnapped?

"Uncle, do you know where we are? I'm so confused."

Uncle. This child.

"I don't know either," I answer with my best professional smile.

I understand the panic, technically. Waking up in a sealed room with strangers isn't something a reasonable person takes well. What am I even saying. I got snatched and dropped in here, and I'm just doing a solid impression of someone who's fine. Inside it's a different story.

Scanning the room, a few other people have the same look I probably have. Arms down, mouths shut, eyes moving. The crying woman from the restaurant is here too. She's given up on tears. She leans against the far wall with that dead-eyed look people get when they've burned through everything and come out empty, her gaze fixed on nothing in particular.

The smell is already bad. Too many people in too little space, and the air is going stale fast. There are no children in the room, which means whoever set this up at least has a minimum age policy. Fourteen-year-old me would've been left out, which he'd have been bitter about.

---

["Greeting Human!"]

["Welcome to the tutorial!"]

---

A loud voice fills the room and everything goes quiet for maybe 2 seconds before the cursing starts. The voice is womanly, calm, coming from nowhere visible, and the room takes that personally.

---

["Please prepare yourself!"]

["Tutorial chapter 1 will start in 2 hours, enjoy!"]

---

Ah. So this might be a—

"FUCK! IS THIS A PRANK!?"

My eardrums. The guy looks exactly like the type of customer who makes restaurant work miserable: drunk-lean, red face, voice already at max. He screams one slur after another and about half the room joins in behind him like they've been waiting for a signal.

Those idiots. You stay quiet when you've been taken. Low profile is always the move, at least for me. Making noise burns through the air faster and gives whoever's watching you exactly what they want.

While the room fills with noise I glance at the girl standing beside me. She's still calm, which is more than I'd expect from a teenager in a sealed box.

"What's your name, kid?"

"Ah, uncle. I'm Soo-Yun."

"Asian? How old are you?"

"I'm 16."

So 16 is the cutoff. They really should've pushed that to 18. She's too young for a sealed room with a ticking clock on the air supply.

"Uncle, why don't you join the protest?"

"Why would I? There's no door in this room," I say, keeping my voice flat. No exit, no vent, nothing that opens. The shouting doesn't change that.

"Do you want candy, kid?"

"My parents told me not to take things from strangers."

"Do you want it?"

"Yes."

"Here. Don't let anyone see it."

I might die in here anyway. Not much point in holding onto things.

Am I really that unlucky? Twenty-six years old, zero relationships, and now this. The crying woman at the restaurant had at least loved someone hard enough to break her heart over him. I haven't even gotten to that part yet.

"Do you remember where you were before you ended up here?"

"I was on my way home and then I blacked out. Then I was here."

"Same here. Also, stop calling me uncle. I'm 26."

"Then what should I call you, uncle? I'm not very good with strangers."

"Call me Eric."

Generic enough. There's no way I'm telling a 16-year-old my actual name. I've never liked it anyway.

"Oh, sir Eric."

"Sir... never mind."

The protest is still going and it's been close to 30 minutes. I have no idea where they're getting the energy from. Either something stimulant-related or a condition that won't let them stop. Either way, it's not my problem, and burning their energy like this isn't going to open the walls.

I lean my head back against the wall and look up at the ceiling. Whoever painted it should find a different career. Soo-Yun is still watching the protest like it's going to end differently if she keeps an eye on it. The room is about the size of a lecture hall, and staring up at the ceiling drags back about four years of memories from rooms exactly like this one, which leads me directly to thinking about the degree I stopped using 2 years after graduation. Great use of time. Really nailed that investment.

Closing my eyes, the dark comes easy.

My parents were religious. Not casually religious. The kind where devotion is a full-time job and God is the answer to every question, and living correctly means following a set of instructions someone else wrote about what your life is worth. I spent a long time sitting through it, trying to absorb it, trying to locate whatever they were feeling.

That eventually stopped working.

I don't hate religion. I don't go out of my way to argue with anyone who believes something or disrespect what they've built their life around. What I couldn't keep doing was treating my own life as a debt owed to something I couldn't confirm. The version of faith I was raised with wanted me to be grateful for a life I didn't ask for and terrified of wasting it, and at a certain point I couldn't hold both of those things at once anymore.

So I made my own rules. Just for me. No group, no document, no formal structure. I decided what was acceptable in my life and what wasn't, and I accepted full responsibility for whatever follows from that. If someone wants to argue that's not real religion, fine. Every major religious text ever written was put down by a person. I'm a person too. The logic holds.

My rules are simple. Do what I want, let other people do what they want, don't impose and don't accept imposition. If there's something after death, I'll find out when I get there. I'm not in a rush toward it but I'm not afraid of it either.

That last part isn't entirely true. I've wanted to die before, in a low-level, background-noise kind of way. Nothing dramatic. The option has always been on the table and I've never found a reason compelling enough to take it off. Nothing in my life has felt like a real loss if I left. No one waiting, nothing unfinished.

My one regret, lying there with my eyes closed and the shouting fading out around me, is that I never got around to loving someone. Even the crying woman had that.

The dark behind my eyes pulls me down slowly.

***

"Uncle, are you awake?"

Soo-Yun's face is way too close when I open my eyes. I'm apparently still alive.

"How long did I sleep, Soo-something?"

"My name's Soo-Yun!"

"Right. How long?"

"About an hour, uncle."

"I'm impressed the oxygen held that long." I look past her at the crowd, which is somehow still going. An hour of sustained screaming and they've still got fuel in them. Impressive in the most useless possible way.

---

["Tutorial Chapter 01 Starting In 1 Minute!"]

---

About time.

"Kid, stay close to me."

I get up and roll my shoulders. The room shifts as everyone rises. The woman from the restaurant gets up from the wall slowly, eyes still raw, moving like she's running on nothing.

---

["3!"]

["2!"]

---

'Alright. A little nervous.'

---

["1!"]

---

'Life is adventure, I guess.'

---

["Welcome to Tutorial Chapter 1, enjoy!"]

---

No further explanation. The thick walls go transparent all at once, glass-clear, and on the other side is a forest: tall trees, dense undergrowth, the kind of quiet that means something is already watching.

"The fuck?" someone says nearby, and I agree. A forest, out of everything. Wouldn't have predicted that.

"Come on, kid. Follow me."

I walk through the glass wall with the rest of them because whoever built this setup clearly wants us outside. Some people stay back. That's their choice. I don't.

A few steps into the trees I slow down. When life has already thrown enough at you, weird stops registering as surprising. But the thing standing in the clearing ahead of me is a different category.

A kappa, about 1.5 meters tall, greenish-grey, shell still wet and dripping. It's holding a machete with the grip of something that has used one before.
